title: Lesson 297
transcript: We've discussed the defining characteristics of cloud. Now let's talk about the different cloud service models, deployment models, and some of the roles related to cloud computing. Now let's start with the service models. You've probably heard of these before. You've heard of infrastructure as a service, platform as a service, software as a service. But let's provide nice, simple, concise definitions of each one and what they provide to you. Because fundamentally, the cloud service models are what services are being offered in the cloud and what services can we consume as a consumer. So let's start with perhaps the easiest to understand software as a service. Software as a service is this concept that we can rent access to an application that is provided in the cloud. Typically, it's a web based application. And this rental part is important because this is a big shift in how we use software. It used to be software was on premise and we would pay for licenses or we would buy the software. With software as a service in the cloud, one of the big defining characteristics is that you are simply renting access, usually on a monthly basis, or maybe it's even free, to access this software, this application in the cloud. That's all software as a service is. What about infrastructure as a service? We've talked about how in the cloud, we don't have access to any physical hardware, but we have access to all of these virtual resources, compute, network, and storage. So the idea behind infrastructure as a service is you can essentially lift and shift your entire physical data center and move it to the cloud. So all the components that you'd have in a physical data center, firewalls, routers, switches, servers, IDS, IPS appliances, DLP appliances, you can lift and shift all of these physical components to the cloud, but instead of being a physical server or a physical router or a physical switch, it's going to be a virtual router or a virtual switch or a virtual server. And so the idea of infrastructure as a service is you can recreate your entire infrastructure as virtual components in the cloud. So that's the concept of infrastructure as a service. Now the final one is sometimes the most confusing for people. Platform as a service. Let's say that there's, you wanna have access to some software in the cloud and you've looked at all the different software as a service vendors and no one just quite provides the software that you need. So you decide you're gonna have to create your own application. That's what Platform as a Service is for. Platform as a Service provides essentially two major capabilities. It provides a development environment where you can code, create your own application, and also a runtime environment where you can actually run that application. So platform as a service allows users, individuals, organizations to create their own custom applications and then run those applications in the cloud. And oftentimes, when you're using software as a service, for instance, what you're actually paying for is some company out there has seen a niche, has seen an opening in the marketplace. They've created, through platform as a service, their own custom application, and then they start renting access to that application to other people. And so from those consumer's perspective, that actually looks like software as a service. So a lot of the software as a service that's out there, the way it's built is on top of platform as a service. Now, you can find all sorts of edge cases and different types of services that blur the lines between these things, but these are the basic definitions. And there's actually one more that I'll add because it's becoming so incredibly popular nowadays, and that's the idea of containerization or containers as a service. The idea where you place your code, your application within one of these standardized modular containers like a Docker, and then you can run that container in a containerization environment. And so a lot of the big cloud providers nowadays have this concept of containers as a service. This next slide that I'm showing you to you here, there's really only one thing that I want you to look at and think about. You absolutely do not have to memorize any of the words in any of the boxes. What's important is the color of the boxes, the pink versus the purple. And what you'll see here is in infrastructure as a service, there's a lot more pink boxes. And in software as a service, there's almost no pink boxes. They're all purple. What's the coloring mean? The purple boxes are where it's the cloud service provider's responsibility to do these things. So anything related to physical security, physical devices, the underlying hardware compute nodes, even the hypervisor, all of these components are very clearly going to be the cloud service provider's responsibility. Now, what's important to understand here is that where does the consumer have the greatest degree of control? Where do they have the most control over the cloud environment, the greatest ability to put in place their own controls, have their own governance over the cloud, et cetera? And the answer is obviously in infrastructure as a service. And this makes sense because in infrastructure as a service, a consumer can create their own entire network. They can create their own virtual servers. They can install their own operating systems, configure them exactly as they want them, install their own applications. They have a huge amount of control of the environment. Whereas at the other end of the spectrum, software as a service, the end user has, the consumer has essentially very little control of the environment. There's one other really important thing that I want to highlight related to this diagram, and that is that you should really focus your time as a security professional on the boundaries, where there's shared responsibility. Because you're probably familiar with this, whenever there's shared responsibility, if there's multiple people that are responsible for something, who's doing it? And the answer is usually, no one. Because if it's a shared responsibility and it's not clearly documented who's doing what, then there's lots of finger pointing and no one ends up doing it. And so this is something I want to highlight related to cloud security that's so extremely important because most cloud is outsourced, outsourcing to a service provider. So you have to be very, very clear who is going to be doing what, who's responsible for what when you have a service provider. Let's look at a couple examples. In software as a service, who's responsible for access control? Is it the consumer or the service provider? And the answer is, it's a shared responsibility. The service provider is responsible for creating the security kernel, for creating a secure network and operating system and all the components underneath that. That is absolutely the service provider's responsibility. But what about creating accounts and giving those accounts appropriate rights and reviewing the access of those accounts and removing those accounts when they need to be removed? Who's responsible for that? Obviously the consumer, only they can know that. So this is a perfect example of a shared responsibility. And it's critically important to very clearly define who's responsible for what. And ultimately, who maintains the accountability? The consumer. Now let's talk about the deployment models. Deployment models is essentially where is the actual hardware? I mean, a cloud doesn't just magically run in the cloud, it has to run on top of some hardware. So deployment models is essentially about where is that hardware? In the public cloud, it means the hardware is at a service provider and it's entirely their equipment and they have control over it. And who has access to the public cloud? Everyone. So public cloud is the most commonly used type of cloud, but it's also the least trusted because it's entirely run by a service provider and it's accessible by anyone. And there are absolutely lots of attackers, malicious individuals, threat agents on the big cloud service providers. They love their systems. So that's public cloud. The other end of the spectrum is private cloud. Private cloud implies that it's only accessible by one consumer. So public cloud means it's accessible by anyone. Private cloud is accessible by only, it's dedicated or accessible to only one consumer. Where is the hardware actually stored? This is where it gets very confusing. Private cloud could be in an organization's own data center with their own systems, entirely private, could be provided by a service provider, can even be things like VPCs, don't get into all that just think of the basic levels public cloud accessible by everyone less trusted private cloud dedicated to one customer therefore more trusty trustworthy You've also got community cloud. A community cloud is where a small number of organizations that have similar needs get together and build a small cloud that they all share. A perfect example of this, and in fact, the largest example I'm aware of of a community cloud in the world is the US GovCloud. The US government has this requirement that any of their agencies that are using the cloud, that cloud has to be FedRAMP compliant. Well, the U.S. federal government went to Amazon and said, hey, we'd like you to make your AWS environment FedRAM compliant. And Amazon said, no thanks, but we'll create an entire dedicated community cloud just for the U.S. government that will be FedRAM compliant. And that's exactly what they did. And so GovCloud is a community cloud accessible only by US government agencies. And then the final thing here, deployment model, is what's known as hybrid. And hybrid is simply any combination of the other three. Commonly, it's a combination of private and public. And that's where I'll introduce one final term, and that is cloud bursting. Cloud bursting is the idea that if you run out of resources in, say, your private cloud, you can burst into the public cloud and run some of your systems and store data, say, in the public cloud. It doesn't have to be private to public. It's simply the idea of you run out of resources in one cloud, so you burst into another cloud. But that's the idea of cloud bursting. Now let's talk about some of the roles related to cloud computing. It's extremely important to understand who's responsible and accountable for what. And there's a few roles that you'll have to know about. And frankly, these are very easy to understand. You first of all got your cloud consumer. This is the person or organization that is consuming, using the cloud services. You have your cloud processor. This is the service provider that's providing the services. Could be a third party, could even be another business unit within the same company that's providing the cloud services. there's then all sorts of cloud service partners. And the only really major partner that you need to know about are brokers. Brokers act as intermediaries between a consumer and a cloud service provider. And one of the major things that you need to know about cloud brokers is that they don't offer new or unique services on their own, but they can layer services on top of an existing cloud service provider. I'll give you a perfect example. You've probably heard of this concept, a cloud access service broker, CASBs. A cloud broker can sit between a consumer and the cloud provider. And an example of that would be, let's say that a company has decided that all their employees can use Box. Now, the company may not trust sending their data to Box and having Box encrypt the data, because if that was the setup, if Box was compromised, the attacker would have both the encrypted data and the encryption keys, because if Box was doing the encryption, both of those pieces of data would be there. So the company might decide, you know what, we do want to use Box, we want to have that capability for our employees, but we don't want Box performing the encryption. And we also want to, you know, have a little bit of control over what data is being sent to Box. implement some DLP, data loss prevention type capabilities. So what an organization might do is use a cloud service broker that would sit between the consumer and Box. And one of the key things that the broker could do is they could layer on a service of encryption. So when a consumer goes to send data to Box, it would be intercepted by the broker, the data would be encrypted at the broker, and then just the ciphertext would be sent to Box. And now if Box is compromised, all the attacker has is the ciphertext and not the keys. So that's an example where you might use brokers. And one final piece that we have absolutely already talked about but is worth reiterating here. It's this difference between accountability and responsibilities. When organizations outsource data, processes, systems to the cloud, Who maintains the accountability? Can an organization outsource the accountability they have for protecting data, for instance? And the answer is absolutely not. You cannot outsource accountability. You can only outsource responsibility. And so this is why it's so incredibly important that organizations are very clear on what their accountabilities are and very clearly defining what their cloud provider needs to be responsible for to protect the data of the systems appropriately because the accountability remains with the owner.
